title: The Macro Minute: Is the US government shutdown exacerbating the Powell Fed’s policy mistake?

Happy Friday out there team42. It's your
skipper here, Darius Dell to present our
macro minute for Friday, November 7th,
2025. As always, we'll start with the
executive summary from today's leadoff
morning note. So, let's hop right in.
So, today's key macro question is, is
the US government shutdown exacerbating
the PAL Fed's policy mistake? The short
answer is yes.
In terms of the analytical nuance, the
decline in equities today is truncating
the S&P 500's 3-week winning streak.
Losses in cryptocurrencies are deepening
amid a steep sell-off that left digital
assets nearly flat for 2025. This is
after EPFR data indicating US equity
funds posted an eighth straight week of
inflows, the year's longest streak. The
upside capillation that we called for
back in early May, appears to have
concluded and left the positioning cycle
in a dangerous place. per our November
4th leadoff morning note. To the latter
point, our positioning model indicates
there is high risk of a crash in risk
assets over the medium to long term as
evidenced by the quantity of stable
indicators in the model breaching their
respective bull market peak thresholds.
Perhaps most concerning is the fact that
these indicators are as currently as
lopsidedly bullish as they have ever
been on a median basis at historical
bull market peaks with the exception of
the July 1998 peak. The PAL Fed clearly
does not understand the role the
institution must play in this
foreturning or to address the K-shaped
economy crisis and markets may punish
them for that and us over the next few
weeks. We currently anticipate the price
will be manageable within the context of
our paradigm C theme, but we could be
wrong about that. Stay tuned. Elsewhere,
US airlines have started cancelling
select flights nationwide as staffing
shortages from the government shutdown
disrupt air travel operations. The US
Transportation Department and the
Federal Aviation Administration ordered
carriers to trim 10% of flight capacity
across 40 major airports to ease
systemic strain. The force reductions in
airline travel lend further support to
our U-shaped economy thesis, which
affords the Fed a clear and obvious
window in which to continue normalizing
monetary policy. Our criticism of the
POW Fed's many policy mistakes has been
well documented and wellreceived by both
investors and the general public. So, we
won't beat that deceased equin again.
Rather, we turn our attention to our
friend, Black Rockck Executive Rick
Reer, who is on Treasury Secretary Scott
Besson's short list of potential
successors to chair Jerome Pow. Like
Secretary Bessant, I've known Rick for
many years and consider him to be at the
very top of Global Wall Street from both
a capability and compassion perspective.
two skills that will be required to
persuade his potential colleagues on the
FOMC to ditch their failed
backward-looking reaction function and
get on board with ending the three-year
recession for the consumers and small
businesses gasping for economic oxygen
at the bottom of the US's K-shaped
economy. Rick's latest commentary
mirrored our own long-standing
datadriven critique of the historically
tardy POW Fed and stating that the labor
market is weakening and that the policy
rate should decline to 3%. We have a
softening in the labor market that is
quite significant, reader said on
Bloomberg today. If we had the jobs
report number today, I think that would
be reflective thereof. Secretary Besson
Scott, if you're listening, Team 42
cannot endorse Rick enough for the
fourth turning size job of implementing
structural regime change at the Fed.
Much needed structural regime change at
the Fed. This institution with its
myriad of hundreds of econs
continues to fail the American public.
And again, this is not a tirade against
econs. I have a tremendous amount of
respect for anybody that can attain a
doctorate in economics. It's very hard.
I remember the first class I took at
Yale. I walked into my microeconomics
course and I and literally I thought I
was reading Chinese and I walked out of
the course early. I called my mom and I
said, "Mom, I'm not sure I'm going to be
here for very long." Um, I this true
story. So, uh, clearly these people are
obviously incredibly bright. They're
brilliant. This is again not a not a a
porative attack on econs, but what it is
is an attack on their hubris and it's
attack on their illusion of validity. uh
it's their attack on their ability to
think that they know all the answers
when in fact they should be
demonstrating a far more humility and
have a much more open mind about how to
address some of these issues that we
continue to see in the economy and asset
markets. I mean half the economy if not
more from a sectoral and household
perspective and business perspective has
been in a recession for three almost
three plus years now and they they have
no idea the labor market is very clearly
unraveling no matter where you turn.
Obviously, they've got the Challenger
job cut data, the sharpest uh decline in
challenger job cuts in the month of
October since 2003.
You know, again, I'm just cherrypicking
that data point, but there's been so
many other data points going back to the
data points that I cited in the op-ed
that I wrote in late July. And so for
the POW Fed to be pushing back against
further normalization of their allegedly
restrictive policy, which they continue
to confirm is restrictive is uh is is is
ironic and noteworthy in my opinion,
especially given the context of the uh
stress that is building on a trend basis
uh in the repo market. No matter what
direction you turn, there's there's all
sorts of signals from both the economy
and financial markets that are telling
the Fed that they are falling further
behind the curve as it relates to their
monetary policy and their balance sheet
policy.
And so it's I I I can't I don't
understand what what the what what the
what the holdup is. Um I hope it's not
political. I really do hope it's not

definitely a risk. Uh, and so I I I
don't know. That's I don't know. I don't

operating that institution. You know, I
think they just need to demonstrate a

about the legacy of policy mistakes
which we talked about uh in last

know, it's a very long list of very
hazardous policy mistakes that have left
a significant scarring uh in and across
uh the US economy. So, as always,
members of our global investor community
can trust that our institutional grade
risk management overlays, KISS and Dr.
remote will help our portfolios navigate
these emergent risks better than
investors attempting to manage risk
without systematic rules or relying on
fundamental research alone.
Transitioning to our 42 micro dashboard,
as always, we'll wrap up with a question
from our community. This one's titled
Supreme Court tariff rule. Dar Darius,
after reading the posturing of both
conservative and liberal judges on the
Supreme Court towards Trump's attorney,
I'm almost certain that they're going to
rule against him on his ability to
implement the tariffs under the
emergency powers act. Something I agree
with. The decision is expected sometime
between December and June of 2026. How
would you expect this to impact the
markets? I'm actually supportive of
Trump's use of tariffs to attempt to
equalize the trade imbalances, but I
recognize the pattern of questioning by
the justices and I'm willing to bet that
on their decision to rule against him.
Just very curious to know your thoughts
on the ramifications if indeed it plays
out uh that way. So, a few things I'll
say about this. Number one, earmuffs. If
you hear the word tariffs, I've been
telling you guys that since May,
early May, May 3rd to be exact. If you
hear the word tariffs, put on ear muffs
because the media is going to overplay
this because it is negative and the
media gets paid to sell you negativity
because it sells it moves ad product
faster than positivity. This is not an
attack on the financial media. This is
literally 100% of all forms of media.
100% of all forms of media be it social,
TV, print, they all make money by
selling advertising. And selling
negative stories sells more advertising
and generates more revenue for their
businesses, businesses, media businesses
than selling positive stuff. So, it's
been my job as a macro risk manager all
year to focus our global investor
community on the positive stuff. If you
think about the Trump economic agenda,
it's a four-legged stool. Besson said it
was a three-legged stool, but in my
opinion, it's a four-legged stool. Uh,
on one side of the ledger, the negative
one, the tariff one that keeps making
news because everyone's so focused on
the orange man and his truth social
account. That's the one that keep
everyone's been focused on that's caused
him to
dramatically underperform both the
markets and Kiss and Dr. Mo all year
because they've been hyperfocused on the
one negative leg of the stool. The other
three legs of the stool are resoundingly
bullish. You have fiscal easing, you
have deregulation, and you have likely
structural regime change at the Fed
that'll lead to monetary easing. You
know, we could be wrong about the
latter, but the reality is on a net
basis, fiscal easing plus substantial
deregulation plus monetary easing is
going to dramatically overwhelm the
tariff, the negative part of the
economic agenda, because trade and the
good sector are not big parts of the US
economy, which is something I've been
saying all year. So, we obviously nailed
that more than anybody. I like I said, I
continue to say there hasn't been a
person, a firm on Global Wall Street
that's been more right about
macroeconomic and financial market risk

in April 2021 against any investor in

know, you know, some, you know, with
someone having some super high beta uh
uh crypto exposure that's blown up two

this firm in April of 2021. So uh
getting back into this um Supreme Court

tariffs. Uh section 301 tariffs which
